comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's coming up on midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is the best image I could find of that force of nature, T. Galen Hieronymus, inventor of the Hieronymus Machine and electroshamanic cult figure extraordinaire. He's one of the many people in modern industrial society who discovered the thing you're not supposed to discover, the thing Carl von Reichenbach (last week's occult poster child) called Od -- the thing that Chinese martial artists call qi, Hindu yogins call prana, Druids call nwyfre, and characters in my fantasy novels call voor. Yes, we're talking about the life force. Hieronymus called it eloptic energy, because he found that it behaved like electricity in some ways and like light (i.e., optically perceived electromagnetic energy) in others, and he built a series of remarkable electronic contraptions to work with it. Legendary SF editor John Campbell discovered, in turn, that the Hieronymus Machine still worked when you swap out the electronic components for a paper circuit diagram, proving that Hieronymus had invented the world's first tunable talisman. Yes, I have one in my study...

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar. 

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions -- and yes, this means you. See you next week!***

What is your take on "Smartest Guy in the Room Syndrome"?

I've noticed it is pervasive in collegiate types, especially those who teach professionally. Personally, I tend to really enjoy these types in small doses. They are willing to argue, which is usually fun. That said, serious issues always prop up, given enough time.

First, I've noticed the SGINTRs tend to confuse their own rather rigid thought-structures for The Truth, and so often will argue things which are incoherent. They also try to shoehorn things that can only be approached with awe into things that can be grasped by the human mind, which I find impious. Usually they have one or two hobby horses that they try to relate everything to regardless of relevance. Given all of this, they are oddly easily swayed when another person uses a flashy and bold style to present a line of thinking. They are effortlessly easy to con, but paradoxically display excellent sense in sniffing out marks.

All of this combines with poor personal boundaries, a drearily didactic bearing, and often what I can only describe as lousy personal hygiene. They seem to fall into treating whomever they relate to as a student, even if that is not in any way a part of the relationship.

replies.title: How not to get SMITR syndrome:
replies.content:
Way #1: Take the multifacteted Adult Intelligence test and get a complete profile. If there is one great peak, subtract that and get the average without it. You can drop from 143 to 108 immediately.

Item #2: Fail in the workplace. Time after time. While every Jane, Juanita, and Johnetta knows exactly how to handle things. Go to lunch with them and LISTEN!

OR: Get your tail in a bear trap you can't reason your way out of, while the Admin or the janitor has the clue. Or every mail handler on the mailroom floor. Rinse & repeat. Or learn and practice a trade. A manual trade.

Be told some home truths by someone you really respect and can't brush off as "just jealous.

Get to an age when if the Ugly Duckling were gong to sprout swan feathers, she would have already, and what you have is a cackling old hen, still singing (in a calypso rhythm) "I wonder why nobody don' like me?"

Have a priest or priestess with a very strong streak of hard Appalachian or Midwestern country sense and no compunctions about explaining the facts of life to you.

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection
replies.content: Delighted to hear it. In Catholicism the sacred direction is east -- that's the direction you face in a traditionally designed Catholic church when you're facing the altar during Mass. Still, if facing out the window works for you, face out the window!

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection
replies.content: Just to add on to JMG's point: "ad orientem" is the direction of worship and prayer for all of the more ancient forms of Christianity (at least the ones I'm familiar with), including Catholicism, Orthodoxy, the non-Chalcedonian churches, etc. Lots of explanations for why this is have been offered retroactively (Christ rising like the sun from the dead, for instance, or patiently awaiting the eschatological Day of the Lord), but the actual origin of this practice is lost to history. Still, whatever its genesis, if you're working within a sacramental Christian tradition, East is the direction of choice :)

replies.title: Re: Sphere of Protection
replies.content: Another datum: The Protestant church I grew up in also had its altar and baptismal font to the East, although I'm not sure whether that was intentional or simply a natural consequence of the fact that the road it's adjacent to is on the west.

comments.title: Talismans

Hi JMG,

I had asked this on the open post but I think my question got lost amongst others as I didn't see a response. Forgive me if I missed it or if I'm repeating myself unnecessarily.

After consecrating a talisman via the Circles of Power rituals, instructions are given to put it in a safe container such as an envelope which won't block etheric energies. Before the end of the ritual, its advised to keep the talisman in silk or linen to protect its etheric energies.

Does the talisman stay wrapped in silk/linen when its put inside the other container/envelope after consecration?

Conner

replies.title: Re: Talismans
replies.content: Nope. That would limit its capacity to radiate those energies and have an effect on the world. You slide it out of the cloth and into the envelope, and then put it somewhere where you're not doing banishing rituals -- preferably in the bottom of a drawer or the back of a closet, where it won't be disturbed.

replies.title: Re: Talismans
replies.content: If you're using the ritual in Circles of Power, or any other Golden Dawn-style working, remember that a little of your own vital energy is going into that talisman. I don't recommend having more than three talismans working at any one time, to prevent that from making you tired and ill.

comments.title: Greek deities for Spirit Below
comments.content:
I've been doing the SOP a couple of months, but I'm stuck about moving on to Spirit Below because I don't know which deity to invoke. I do feel pretty certain that Dionysos is probably the wrong god for me personally to be invoking at the moment, and I am sure about not using his name for Spirit Below. But then, when I try to find alternatives, I get into what might be overthinking.
I am already invoking Demeter for Earth. (I feel Artemis to be about being on the surface of the earth and movement on it, rather than the stillness of the Earth element.)
Persephone? I'm not sure she's below enough, as she's only there for half the year, though it is at least coming up for fall.
Hekate? You shouldn't mess around with Hekate and I'm not one of those people who's otherwise ever felt the urge to.
You already said a few weeks ago to another questioner that Hades wasn't a good choice and likewise he isn't someone I am comfortable with invoking.
Gaia? The mythology shows her in conflict with Apollo, who is a presence I am certain I want in the SOP...but then a lot of the Greek deities have conflicts with each other.
I'm familiar with the Apollo-Dionysos binary but as it only seems to date from Nietzsche I don't feel it's totally necessary.

replies.title: Re: Greek deities for Spirit Below
replies.content:
Okay, first, the Apollo-Dionysos binary wasn't original to Nietzsche; it shows up in the writings of Robert Fludd a good two centuries before Nietzsche got there. (I'm not sure where Fludd got it.)

What I'd recommend, if you don't want to work with Dionysos, to move Apollo to the East -- as the god of light and intellect, he's well suited to the eastern quarter -- and Zeus to Spirit Above. Then invoke Gaia as Spirit Below.

Yes, this will require to back up a bit. Tomorrow, just do Air, invoking Apollo; proceed to add in the others, one per day; then spend your week on Spirit Below, and go on from there.

comments.title: Reincarnation
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I've noticed something a bit strange lately, and I'd like a sanity check...

On my blog, and in some private fora, I've been discussing the concept of reincarnation as it's introduced in Bardddas - and also on your blogs, I think.

Briefly, the purpose of reincarnation is that the spirit, on its long journey from Annwn to Gwynfyd, slowly progresses through ever-more complex forms of life, each with a slightly greater capacity for abstract thought, self-awareness, and moral choice. The spirit will incarnate many times in each species until it has fully explored, and mastered, that species' full ability to exercise these attributes. Humans are the species with the greatest potential capacity to use them; master everything humans can do with them, and it's onwards to the non-corporeal forms of existence.

Now, I accept it's likely I've misinterpreted Barddas, and that I've misunderstood what you've written on the topic... but don't *think* so. Equally likely is that I'm simply not communicating the concept clearly.

So, given those caveats, I find it very strange that on this topic alone, I get huge pushback. I keep getting comments that this puts humans on a pedestal, even if I explain that this is not about which species is 'most important' in the ecosphere. I get the feeling, though I haven't asked, that many of the people who protest about this are happy to accept reincarnation and karma in Buddhism, but don't want to see it in contemporary Druidry - even though it's been there from the beginning...

I wonder if it is maybe a reaction of refugees from Christianity against the concept of man being master of the world - which, of course, is not at all what it's saying. Or, perhaps, it's a dislike for the "actions have consequences" or "spiritual development is a path with a destination, and it requires change and growth" suggestions. Don't know.

Anyway, I found it odd that this concept was meeting such resistance, and I was wondering if you've encountered it, and/or have any comments about it?

Diolch yn fawr,

Bogatyr

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content:
My experience is lots of people are fine with "funny, clueless foreigners" holding ideas like karma, but how can "educated, intelligent Westerners"?

Then there's also the fact that asking them to accept actions have consequences hits a fairly major sore spot for a lot of people these days....

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation
replies.content:
I've gotten the same thing. I think the thing that puts people's nerves on edge is that in the teachings of Barddas, this stage of spiritual evolution is crunch time: this is the point where you have to measure up to a certain standard, or tumble back down into animal existences to try again. People don't like that, because it means that what they do really does matter, and they can fail.

The one thing I've found that sometimes works is to point out that humans aren't the only incarnate life form at this same level -- whales and porpoises have the same approximate level of sentience, and so the souls embodied in them are facing the same challenge we are. They're doing it under slightly different conditions, as cetacean bodies aren't equipped to manipulate their environments the way ours are, but that doesn't change the basic test: do you waken into self-knowledge and realize (not just think or decide) that you are your higher self, not the mass of fossilized passions and fears most of us think of as our identity? Or do you fail to do so -- or, worse, refuse the identification?

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation

Bogatyr

replies.title: Re: Slavic deities
replies.content:
A word of caution here: there wasn't ever a single original Slavic pantheon. Different Slavic peoples, to judge by the very skimpy available evidence, worked with non-overlapping sets of Gods and Goddesses borrowed from other ethnicities. Modern Slavic Pagans generally cobble together their pantheons out of all these sources, heavily supplemented by various entities--who are not necessarily either all that old or Divine--known from recent folklore (which also varies considerably from one Slavic people to the next).

The best documented pantheon is the one set up by King Vladimir in Kiev and his uncle Dobryña in Novgorod in the second half of the 10th century. Some of its deities seem to be Slavic in their origin; others have pretty clearly been borrowed from forms of "Iranian" (in the broad sense) religious culture. Vladimir and his royal family had come to Russia from Scandinavia (probably Sweden) two or three generations back.

There's a helpful article on the deities Vladimir enshrined in his temple in Kiev, and they can be assigned easily to the stations of the SoP:

Water: Dazhbog
Fire: Simargl
Earth: Mokosh
Spirit Below: Veles
Spirit Above: Perun

replies.content:
It's a quite reasonable take on Vladimir's pantheon, though other arrangements could also be supported. I might have put Svarog instead of Simargl (about whom we know absolutely nothing) for fire. Mokosh is probably the best God(dess) for Earth, and Stribog is almost certainly the best God for air. Dazhbog isn't associated with water, but with the Sun and light. Perhaps one might interchange him and Perun. since thunderstorms are an impressive sort of rainstorm.

In the original sources, the images set up by Vladimir on a hill next to his palace in Kiev numbered six (or maybe seven, since some scholars divide the name Simargl into two names, Sima and Regl).

They are Perun, Stribog, Dazhbog, Khors, Simargl and Mokosh. Only Perun (thunderstorms), Stribog (wind), and Dazhbog (the sun and light) are characterized at all in any source. Perun seems to have been the best known of them all, and the most widely venerated by the common people (like Thor in Scandinavia). Dobryña, King Vladimir's uncle, set up an image of Perun in Novogorod, but didn't bother with the others on the list.

The other three (or four) are just bare names in a single list in a very old chronicle. Mokosh seems to be in the feminine grammatical gender, and might be the fertile Earth; the others are all in the masculine gender.

Veles, the God of Cattle (or Wealth), is not part of this pantheon; his image was elsewhere in Kiev, apparently down off the hill, in the city marketplace. So he works well as Spirit Below.

A God well attested in Old Russian texts, but not included in Vladimir's pantheon, was Svarog--possibly connected with fire and the forge. In one translation from the Greek, the name of the God Hephaistos is translated as Svarog (and Helios as Dazhbog, IIRC).

On the other hand, Perun and Volos appear as a linked pair of Gods by whom Pagan Russians swore to uphold treaties, so it is elegant to place them as Spirit Above and Spirit Below, despite the problems this makes for the resulting schema with Dazhbog as water.

comments.title: Exploring Esoteric Christianity
comments.content:
Hi JMG! I'm sorry to see that you're feeling grumpy today, or were last night... thank you for indulging our questions regardless. :)

I have, despite my best intentions, spent my adult life as a spiritual dilettante. I rotate between trying to come to terms with Christianity; becoming disgusted with the organized churches and orthodox theologies and throwing myself into attempting to acquire a non-Christian spiritual identity in response to that disgust; failing utterly at that because of a lack of personal resonance for the cultural accouterments of the other practices (I have a degree in Anthropology and got "thou must not culturally appropriate" pounded into me pretty thoroughly); and giving up temporarily in despair at my apparent incapacity to nurture any sort of organized spiritual life.

The weird thing is, I was raised agnostic? And yet, I still keep coming back to the forceful intuition that somehow, in some form, I need to come to terms with the Christian God. Last week's discussion about the karmic implications of adult baptism suggests why this might be the case, so I am giving it yet another try, and attempting to approach Christianity from a more esoteric direction.

I really enjoyed your essay in "Jesus through Pagan Eyes" about approaching Christ as an avatar of the life force, and have been trying to find resources that expand on this concept with questionable results. It's also possible that I just don't know enough about this stream of religious thought to recognize what I'm looking at. Can you make any recommendations for beginning to research/practice this type of Christianity?

My current practices are limited to daily journaling and a bit of grounding meditation for my anxiety. I am also currently puzzling over what seemed, possibly, to be a genuinely inspired thought that popped into my pen while journaling last week: "The Tree of Life is the body of the Green Christ." No, I don't really know what that means. But it feels important? Anyway, I'd appreciate any insight or suggestions that you or other Christian esotericists among the forum denizens might offer.

replies.title: Re: Exploring Esoteric Christianity
replies.content:
Hmm. Has it ever occurred to you that "thou must not culturally appropriate" is a means of policing the behavior of the privileged? It amounts to "If you belong to the educated classes, you must never learn anything from anyone who doesn't." It's a very effective way of keeping you inside your nice cramped suffocatingly narrow box. (Tip of the hat to Violet, who pointed that out to me some months ago.)

If you want to come to terms with Christianity, you can do that, but you actually have to come to terms with it, not waffle. There's a relevant bit in Revelations 3:15-16: "I know thy works, that thou art neither cold nor hot: I would thou wert cold or hot. So then because thou art lukewarm, and neither cold nor hot, I will spue thee out of my mouth." Be hot or be cold; accept Christianity or reject it, one or the other.

If you choose to accept it, I'd like to suggest the advice I received from one of the most remarkably spiritual people I've ever met, an old lady I knew via lodge connections many years ago. Her advice was to read one chapter of the Bible every day, first thing in the morning, beginning with the Gospel of Matthew. ("Never try to start with Genesis," she liked to say. "You'll get to the begats and quit.") There's a specific way to do it. You start by getting out the Bible, opening it to the chapter you're going to read, and then praying briefly to the Holy Spirit, asking for insight. Then read the chapter. Then stop for a while and think about what you've read, what it teaches, and why it matters. Then pray -- about that, or about anything else that moves you -- finishing up with the Lord's Prayer. Then close the Bible and go on with your day.

The point of faith, after all, isn't about joining a church or becoming part of a tradition. It's about establishing a personal relationship with the god you worship, and that's best done in private. Once you've established a practice of Bible reading and prayer and kept it up daily for a couple of months, you might consider looking for a church in the Liberal Catholic tradition; you might also consider picking up Gareth Knight's book Experience of the Inner Worlds, which is a guide to Christian Cabalistic magic. Bible study and prayer come first, though!

replies.title: Re: Exploring Esoteric Christianity
replies.content:
Thanks; this gives me a lot to consider and journal about.

I think that you and Violet are probably spot on; looking back, my liberal arts "education" didn't really encourage us to take anything beyond our own navels seriously. It also pushed a very post-modern worldview that tacitly punished students who attempted to passionately affirm or reject anything, which seems like an almost deliberate method of creating disengaged, lukewarm graduates.

I can't say it didn't succeed, at least with me. But that isn't who I want to be. Unfortunately, I live in one of those spiritual deserts of homogeneity that you sometimes encounter in the bible belt, and there isn't a liberal anything within 50 miles. So anything I attempt, I have to somehow find the willpower to stick with it without the support of a community. (Willpower isn't really something that was encouraged by my upbringing or education either, come to think of it. I wonder why?)

I suppose that I'm trying to find a "seed concept" of the Christian God that doesn't repel me the way that the orthodox versions do, so that I can focus my prayers and contemplations appropriately in order to build the relationship you mentioned. There certainly seems to be more to Him than that; otherwise so many people could not have loved Him so passionately for so many centuries. But I have to admit that aside from vague intuitive yearnings toward Something More, right now I just don't get it.

Bible reading and prayer are certainly wise and foundational advice. I just have to decouple my distaste for the hellfire and brimstone bible thumpers from the scripture itself... shouldn't be too difficult, right? And then figure out *how* to pray, since even the churches I've attended never seem to bother to teach you that aside from just mouthing the words. I guess it's supposed to be obvious or instinctive or something, but I've never managed anything that didn't feel contrived.

And now I'm whining. Sorry. I'm just frustrated nearly to desperation by my own inability to either fill the proverbial existential void, or to just dismiss it and get on with a life of indifferent secular materialism. Which obviously isn't your problem. I'm going to shut up now, and probably immediately regret actually posting this rambling pity party. Excuse me; must go journal.

replies.title: Re: Exploring Esoteric Christianity
replies.content:
It's not a pity party; it's a serious issue that a lot of people have to wrestle with these days. Prayer, to begin with, is anything but obvious or instinctive -- it's something that even the great mystics have had to learn. It's going to feel contrived, fake, worthless, stupid, fill in the blank -- at first. You have to keep on trying, and bit by bit get past what the old Rosicrucians called "material inclination," the tendency of the unawakened soul to fixate on the sensory life and discount the life of the heart, mind, and spirit.

Establishing a practice of Bible reading and prayer will probably be the hardest thing you will ever do; the payoffs are commensurate. The best advice I've ever received is to simply talk to God, daily, and also listen.

With regard to a concept of God, well, there's a term for that: idolatry. Don't try to decide what God is. Open yourself to him and let him show you what he is, knowing that it won't happen quickly, and it won't happen all at once. That was another reason why my teacher in these matters insisted on beginning with the Gospels; if you start with those, you start with Christ, who is the bridge between you and God.

You're fighting your entire background, you know -- and you're fighting for your life. Take that seriously, and recognize that it matters just as much as it feels like.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
As a man who was not circumcised and who has studied the topic in significant depth from both an ethical and medical perspective, it's pretty clear to me that most, if not all of the reasons for circumcision from that perspective (including the "sand under the foreskin" argument, with all due respect, JMG!) are bogus. It's adoption as a so-called "medical" practice in the English speaking world is primarily due to the Victorian obsession with preventing and punishing masturbation. You can look up John Harvey Kellogg (yes, the inventor of cornflakes!) for his take on the topic.

The religious dimension is harder to parse, though it is clearly very significant as part of puberty-age initiations (for men and women) in some tribal cultures. My take is that the circumcision practices in Judaism and Islam likely originated that way, and then got moved to younger children for a variety of reasons.

I think the moral implications of doing it to an infant should not be understated, though clearly within some religious contexts it's understood differently. I've known men who were circumcised as infants who viewed it as a blood sacrifice done to appease the God of the Hebrew Bible, and they wrestled with it as adults who no longer worshipped that God.

Ok then, that gives me 2 questions.

replies.title: Re: Hieronymus machine
replies.content:

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Not exactly. The word "demon" covers a lot of territory, and includes many different categories of beings. Some are ordinary spiritual beings who happen to be hostile to humans, or simply find us tasty -- think of them as the spiritual equivalents of sharks. Some are human souls that have gotten stuck between lives, accidentally or deliberately, and have taken up a predatory existence in order to avoid going on. Some -- the most dangerous of the lot -- were the inhabitants of universes before ours, who for one reason or another remained behind when those universes wound up and the rest of their inhabitants went on to other things. Cabalistic literature refers to them as qlippoth, "shells" or "shards;" from the perspective of our universe, they're wildly distorted, the Lords of Unbalanced Force. Traditional lore has it that eventually, over vast cycles of time, they'll find their way back to the cycle of creation and redemption, but for our present age and many ages to come they're a serious problem.

comments.title: Healing Affirmation
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I have an internal injury which I have been trying to heal myself (3+ yrs now) and am working on crafting an affirmation to help aid the healing process. This is my first time seriously trying to compose and use an affirmation, and would greatly appreciate your advice. First, a quick bit of background...

My injury is one which every doctor recommends surgery for, but there are accounts of people who have been able to heal themselves. It doesn't cause me any pain, though it does inconvenience me in a number of ways. I've been trying to heal myself through exercise, good diet, precaution... but haven't seen much improvement. Thankfully, it hasn't gotten any worse either. In most other respects, I am in very good health and still fairly young.

The affirmation I've come up with is, "I am healing my [injury], and am so grateful to have such a strong and healthy body".

1) Do you think this is a good formulation, and do you have any recommendations on how it could be improved, or a more appropriate alternative?

2) Could you give me some advice on how best to use such an affirmation, such as when, where, how often, and for how long I should repeat it?

3) Would there be any benefit to touching/massaging the injured area while saying the affirmation? Or anything else I should be doing while repeating it?

Thank you very much in advance for taking the time to answer my questions.

replies.title: Re: Healing Affirmation
replies.content:
1) "I am healing" is imperfect tense, and so you're telling your subconscious to keep healing but never to get around to being healed. The classic approach is to say "I am healed," and to imagine yourself vividly in a state of perfect health, with the injury completely gone. In your place I'd make the statement of gratitude -- which is an excellent idea, btw -- a separate affirmation.

2) In your place I'd repeat the statement of gratitude twenty times silently on rising each morning. Then, at intervals during the day, "I am healed!" Put as much imaginative force as you can into each repetition, and do it at least a hundred times a day. Keep that up for a year and a day.

3) Don't do anything that will draw attention to the actual condition of the injury. You're building a thoughtform and charging it with emotion, so that it can then work down the planes into dense matter. Let that happen.

2) You've mentioned before that "character" is that which carries over from life to life, and the "personality" is the stuff which builds up over a lifetime. Is the base of the personality, the most fundamental, deepest level of it, determined by the ascendant, sun sign and moon sign, those which define the astrological "character"? Is this why two successive incarnations can be very different from each other?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I've never known how seriously to take that. It may be just a convenient way to classify souls.

2) It's more complex than that. Your rising sign in this incarnation was your sun sign in your last incarnation; the rest of your chart reflects the karma you have to deal with as well as the character you bring to it. So your charts from two lives may be wildly different.
